response.description_long:
Warehouses rarely buy all their automation from one vendor. Each robot brand arrives with its own fleet manager, its own map, its own queue — and they don't talk. Orders stall while one fleet idles and another backs up. Palletmind, founded in Atlanta in 2020 by engineers from an AMR manufacturer, addresses this fragmentation with vendor-neutral orchestration software for warehouse robotics. The company is now 61 people across Atlanta and Rotterdam.

The product connects upward to a warehouse's WMS — with productized integrations for Manhattan and Blue Yonder — and downward to each vendor's fleet manager through its API. A task-allocation solver re-plans the whole floor every few seconds, deciding which robot should do which job while balancing travel time, battery state, congestion, and order priority. A shared traffic map manages intersections, aisle segments, dock doors, and charging areas as resources with reservations and priorities, preventing the deadlocks mixed fleets are famous for. Before go-live, a digital twin simulates the floor using telemetry from the actual fleet managers, conveyor PLCs, and WMS confirmations, letting operators replay their busiest day and see throughput before moving a single rack. The twin is calibrated by hindcasting against last week's actual performance, with error published in the go-live report.

Palletmind is used by 3PLs and retailers operating mixed fleets across North America and Europe. 3PLs use the twin to model what happens to the floor when a new client's volume lands; retailers standardize one orchestration layer across DCs that were automated in different eras with different vendors. The operations console provides one screen for the whole floor: live task flow, per-fleet utilization, congestion, and order-aging alerts, with every allocation logged and explainable.

The company charges an annual license per site, sized by robot count, which includes integrations, the twin, and support — no per-task fees. Sites running Palletmind report 20 to 35 percent more picks per hour from the same fleet, and integration timelines of 8 weeks instead of the 6 months a custom middleware build typically takes. One European 3PL running three robot brands across two buildings described the before-state as "three good systems taking turns being the bottleneck"; post-deployment, the same fleet cleared daily volume with two hours of shift to spare.

Distinctively, Palletmind does not replace vendor fleet managers but rather decides what work each fleet gets and referees shared spaces. Its solver decisions are inspectable by design, building trust one explained decision at a time. When a vendor's API is limited, the integration degrades gracefully: fleets Palletmind cannot task directly are modeled in the traffic map and planned around. The company publishes its integration matrix openly and maintains vendor integrations as products that update centrally when fleet manager APIs version.
